coredata.dc:title: PEI-SWNT improves RNAi efficiency in Locusta migratoria via dsRNA injection delivery system
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier Inc. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Inc. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Ren, Qiurong
Zhang, Qian
Liu, Yangyang
Li, Shuai
Zhang, Jianqin
Wang, Yanli
El Wakil, Abeer
Moussian, Bernard
Zhang, Jianzhen
coredata.dc:description: The instability of double-stranded RNA (dsRNA) restricts the application of RNA interference (RNAi) technology in agricultural pest management. Various types of nanocarriers have been developed and employed for the stable delivery of dsRNA. Nonetheless, it remains unclear which type of nanomaterial could deliver dsRNA stably and efficiently for gene knockdown in Locusta migratoria. In this study, we evaluated the ability of three biocompatible and low-toxicity inorganic nanomaterials—polyethylenimine (PEI)-functionalized single-walled carbon nanotube (PEI-SWNT), polyethylenimine-functionalized carbon quantum dots (PEI-CQDs), and layered double hydroxide (LDH)—to bind and stabilize dsRNA. The results revealed that, compared to PEI-CQDs and LDH, PEI-SWNT more effectively protected dsRNA from degradation in locust gut fluids, across various temperatures, and under different pH conditions. Furthermore, we investigated the efficacy of PEI-SWNT/dsRNA complexes in suppressing endogenous genes in locusts through both injection and oral administration methods. Compared to bare dsRNA, PEI-SWNT/dsRNA complexes enhanced RNAi efficiency by up to 46.0 % and increased mortality by up to 39.0 %. Moderate levels of PEI-SWNT could improve the germination rate of wheat, while not affecting leaf growth in the short term. To our knowledge, this study is the first to apply PEI-SWNT inorganic nanomaterials in insects, which provides a foundational basis and compelling evidence for the development of nanomaterial-based nucleic acid pesticides.
coredata.dcterms:subject: RNA interference
dsRNA
Locusta migratoria
Inorganic nanomaterials
PEI-SWNT

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) imaging of PEI-SWNT/dsRNA
Injection of nanomaterial/dsRNAs in L. migratoria
Extract RNA from L. migratoria and perform RT-qPCR analysis
Measurement of wheat germination rate and seedling length
Feeding of PEI-SWNT/dsRNA in L. migratoria
Effect of PEI-SWNT/dsRNA nanoparticle complex on L. migratoria development
Feeding locusts with PEI-SWNT/dsRNA via leaf spraying
The impact of PEI-SWNT on wheat
PEI-SWNT exhibits superior binding affinity and protective capacity for dsRNA
PEI-SWNT could significantly enhance the efficiency of RNAi
Limitations and future outlook of PEI-SWNT application in locusts

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by National Key Research and Development Program of China ( 2022YFE0196200 ), Science, Technology & Innovation Funding (STDF) Authority in Egypt ( 46567 ), Taishan Industrial Leading Talent Program, and Postgraduate Education Innovation Program of Shanxi Province ( 2024TD05 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Combination of dsRNA with nanomaterials. (A) Schematic diagram illustrating dsRNA loading on nanomaterials; (B) The residue ratio of dsRNA to nanomaterials. The length of dsLmCht10 is 529 bp. Error bars represent standard deviations of three biological replicates. Data are presented as mean values ± SEM; (C) The stability of nanomaterials/dsLmCht10 complexes in midgut fluid of L. migratoria; (D) The stability of nanomaterials/dsLmCht10 complexes incubated respectively at 4 °C, 28 °C and 37 °C for 0 d,1 d, 5 d, 10 d. The dsRNA in (C, D) was isolated from dsRNA/nanomaterial particles with 0.1 % SDS.

Characterization of PEI-SWNT/dsRNA complex. (A) TEM images of the PEI-SWNT and PEI-SWNT/dsLmCht10. The filament indicated by the purple arrow represents PEI-SWNT, and the structure indicated by the cyan arrow represents dsRNA; (B) hydrated particle size of PEI-SWNT/dsLmCht10 complexes; (C) Apparent Zeta potential of PEI-SWNT/dsLmCht10 complexes. Error bars represent standard deviations of three biological replicates. Data are presented as mean values ± SEM. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

RNAi efficiency and phenotype of PEI-SWNT/dsLmCht10 which injected to the third instar nymphs of L. migratoria. (A) The RNAi efficiency of locusts which injected with different concentration of dsLmCht10; (B) The mortality of locusts which injected with different concentration of dsLmCht10; (C) The RNAi efficiency of locusts which injected with 5 ng PEI-SWNT/dsLmCht10; (D) The phenotype of LmCht10 which had been silenced. When injected with dsGFP or PEI-SWNT, the third instar locusts successfully molted to the fourth instar. However, when injected with dsLmCht10 or PEI-SWNT/dsLmCht10, the third instar locusts failed to shed the old cuticle, eventually leading to death;(E) The survival rate of locusts which injected with 5 ng PEI-SWNT/dsLmCht10. Error bars represent standard deviations of three biological replicates. Data are presented as mean values ± SEM. Different lowercase letters above the columns indicate significant differences (ANOVA, Tukey‘s test, P < 0.05).

dsLmCht10 taken up by fifth-instar L. migratoria via leaf after dsLmdsRNase2 injected. (A) The phenotype observed after silencing of LmCht10. The dorsal split was typically initiated, and the new cuticle was clearly visible. However, the insects were unable to successfully remove the old cuticle; (B) The RNAi efficiency of locusts that were fed with PEI-SWNT/dsLmCht10 (containing 25 μg dsLmCht10 and 5 μg of PEI-SWNT every day) for 3 days; (C) The mortality of locusts that were fed with PEI-SWNT/dsLmCht10 (containing 25 μg dsLmCht10 and 5 μg of PEI-SWNT daily) for 3 days when 15 μg dsLmdsRNase2 was injected for 30 h. Error bars represent standard deviations of three biological replicates. Data are presented as mean values ± SEM. * above the columns indicate significant differences between the treatment and control (t-tests, P < 0.05).

The impact of PEI-SWNT on wheat. (A) The phenotype of wheat seeds soaked with different concentrations of PEI-SWNT solution for 36 h; (B) Germination rate of wheat seeds soaked in PEI-SWNT solution with different concentrations for 36 h, Error bars represent standard deviations of three biological replicates. Data are presented as mean values ± SEM. Different lowercase letters above the columns indicate significant differences (ANOVA, Tukey‘s test, P < 0.05, n = 30). (C) The length of seedling with different concentrations of PEI-SWNT solution for 3 d.

head.ce:title: PEI-SWNT improves RNAi efficiency in Locusta migratoria via dsRNA injection delivery system

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Shanxi Key Laboratory of Nucleic Acid Biopesticides, School of Synthetic Biology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The instability of double-stranded RNA (dsRNA) restricts the application of RNA interference (RNAi) technology in agricultural pest management. Various types of nanocarriers have been developed and employed for the stable delivery of dsRNA. Nonetheless, it remains unclear which type of nanomaterial could deliver dsRNA stably and efficiently for gene knockdown in Locusta migratoria. In this study, we evaluated the ability of three biocompatible and low-toxicity inorganic nanomaterials—polyethylenimine (PEI)-functionalized single-walled carbon nanotube (PEI-SWNT), polyethylenimine-functionalized carbon quantum dots (PEI-CQDs), and layered double hydroxide (LDH)—to bind and stabilize dsRNA. The results revealed that, compared to PEI-CQDs and LDH, PEI-SWNT more effectively protected dsRNA from degradation in locust gut fluids, across various temperatures, and under different pH conditions. Furthermore, we investigated the efficacy of PEI-SWNT/dsRNA complexes in suppressing endogenous genes in locusts through both injection and oral administration methods. Compared to bare dsRNA, PEI-SWNT/dsRNA complexes enhanced RNAi efficiency by up to 46.0 % and increased mortality by up to 39.0 %. Moderate levels of PEI-SWNT could improve the germination rate of wheat, while not affecting leaf growth in the short term. To our knowledge, this study is the first to apply PEI-SWNT inorganic nanomaterials in insects, which provides a foundational basis and compelling evidence for the development of nanomaterial-based nucleic acid pesticides.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: RNA interference (RNAi) is an in vivo post-transcriptional gene silencing technology guided by double-stranded RNA (dsRNA) (Fire et al., 1998). In recent years, RNAi technology has been frequently used in pest management and control (Jain et al., 2021; Yan et al., 2021). This innovative technique involves the design of specific dsRNA molecules targeted at key genes within pest species, such as those governing growth, development, immunity, molting, and reproduction. RNAi effectively leads to pest mortality by reducing the expression of these critical genes (Ning et al., 2024; Silver et al., 2021). Compared to conventional insecticides, RNAi exhibits lower toxicity and higher specificity (Guo et al., 2022). In contrast to the strategy of integrating exogenous dsRNA into plant genomes to create transgenic plants (Yu et al., 2016), RNAi is considered safer and more readily accepted by the public (Kunte et al., 2020).

Typically, dsRNA is delivered into insect bodies through methods like microinjection or direct feeding (Prentice et al., 2017). However, dsRNA is susceptible to degradation by nucleases in the environment (Bachman et al., 2020). The variation in gene silencing efficiency among insects depends on different target genes and the presence of dsRNA nucleases in the gut or hemolymph (Dhandapani et al., 2020; Fan et al., 2022). For instance, in the case of the L. migratoria, higher RNAi suppression is observed following injection compared to oral delivery, attributed to the degradation of orally administered dsRNA by intestinal dsRNase2 (Cooper et al., 2019; Song et al., 2017). Studies have indicated that the combination of dsRNA with nano-carriers such as natural nanomaterials like chitosan (Zhou et al., 2023), liposomes (Castellanos et al., 2019), organic nano materials like cationic polymer (Xu et al., 2014), guanidine-containing polymers (Parsons et al., 2018), inorganic nano materials like carbon nanotubes (Edwards et al., 2020), carbon quantum dots (Wang et al., 2023) and layered double hydroxides (Jain et al., 2022), can enhance dsRNA stability and improve the efficiency of RNAi. In recent years, inorganic nanomaterials have attracted significant attention in pest control applications due to their excellent stability and monodispersity. According to previous studies, carbon nanotubes (Edwards et al., 2020) and carbon quantum dots (Wang et al., 2023), which possess good biocompatibility, low cytotoxicity, and facile surface modification capabilities, are functionalized with cationic groups to carry positive charges. Compared to the delivery of dsRNA alone, the use of positively charged carbon nanotubes wrapped in dendritic polymers significantly enhanced the knockdown of target genes in Tribolium castaneum, thereby improving the efficacy of RNAi against pests (Edwards et al., 2020). In contrast to chitosan, carbon quantum dots were found to further improve the RNAi efficiency and larval mortality of Aedes aegypti when delivered with dsRNA (Das et al., 2015). Moreover, layered double hydroxides (LDHs), known for their strong stability and inherent positive charges, also self-assemble with dsRNA through electrostatic attraction. Furthermore, LDH was demonstrated to effectively suppress the expression of target genes in the whitefly (Jain et al., 2022). These inorganic nanomaterial delivery systems enhance the stability of dsRNA and promote its diffusion and uptake in target cells, providing a research foundation for the effective application of nucleic acid pesticides.

L. migratoria is a highly destructive migratory agricultural pest that feeds on a variety of major cereal crops, including wheat, corn, sorghum, millet and rice. Currently, the predominant strategy for controlling locust outbreaks relies on chemical insecticides. However, this approach may lead to environmental pollution, harm to beneficial organisms, and the development of pest resistance with long-term use. Therefore, it is necessary to employ the RNAi strategy, with highly specific and environmentally friendly to control locust plagues. The Chitinase 10 (Cht10) and vacuolar-type H+-ATPase α subunit (ATPase5A) genes of the migratory locust are promising target genes for RNAi-based locust control. Silencing LmCht10 during the second to fifth instar stages in locusts leads to difficulties in molting and eventual death (Yang et al., 2016). Meanwhile, LmATP5A is an essential gene for locust survival and midgut development, and its silencing results in locust mortality (Hu and Xia, 2016). Therefore, this study primarily selects these two genes as target genes for the RNAi-based control strategy against the L. migratoria.

In this study, three types of inorganic nanomaterials—carbon nanotubes, carbon quantum dots, and layered double hydroxides—were selected as carriers for dsRNA. The objective is to determine the most suitable inorganic nanomaterial by comparing their stability when combined with dsRNA and their efficiency in gene silencing in locusts. The study ultimately confirmed that modified carbon nanotubes combined with dsRNA can significantly increase the stability of dsRNA in the environment, improve the gene silencing efficiency and mortality rate of locusts, providing an effective method for locust control based on the RNAi strategy.

The L. migratoria, originating from Yunnan province, China, was cultivated in a professional laboratory under controlled conditions. The temperature was maintained at 30 ± 2 °C, with a relative humidity of 40–60 % and adequate lighting for 14 h. Locusts were fed fresh wheat (Triticum aestivum L.) seedlings, which were grown hydroponically in the laboratory under conditions of 16 ± 2 °C temperature and 40 % relative humidity.

Carboxylic acid-functionalized SWNT (single-walled carbon nanotubes) and carbon dots were purchased from Xi'an QiYue Biology. PEI modified SWNT were produced by the heat-driven reaction (Demirer et al., 2019). PEI modified carbon dots were produced by the solvothermal reaction (Schwartz et al., 2020). MgAl-LDH was prepared using MgCl2, AlCl3 and NaOH via a co-precipitation method (Mitter et al., 2017).

The cDNA sequence number of LmATP5A is KC792298.1. The cDNA sequence number of LmCht10 is KU757433.1. The template DNAs and primers of dsLmCht10 and dsLmdsRNase2 and dsLmV-ATPase α used in experiment were ordered from the research group of professor J Z Zhang (Institute of Applied Biology, Shanxi University) (Song et al., 2017). For dsRNA synthesis, the template DNA amplification used a pair of primers modified with a promoter sequence of T7 at the 5′ end (Table 1 ). Then dsRNA was synthesized using 1 μg DNA as template via T7 RiboMAX™ Express RNAi System (Promega, lot: 0000595748), in accordance with protocol. Originally, the system for transcribing dsRNA in vitro consisted of 10 μL Express T7 2 × Buffer, 2 μL Enzyme Mix T7, 1 μg template DNA, and adequate DEPC water to 20 μL, while the procedure being 37 °C for 1.5 h, 70 °C for 10 min and 25 °C for 20 min. Then, any remaining DNA and RNA were removed by treatment with DNase and RNase A, incubating at 37 °C for 30 min. Afterward, the dsRNA was purified by isopropanol sodium acetate, lastly washout with 75 % alcohol. Finally, the dsRNA was dissolved in DEPC water, and its quality was assessed using a 1 % agarose gel.

All nanomaterials, including PEI-SWNT, CQDs, and MgAl-LDH, comply with a consistent procedure for loading dsRNA onto them. Initially, 500 ng of dsRNA was slowly added to a solution containing the specified mass of nanomaterials. The mixture was then gently mixed with a pipette and incubated at 28 °C for 30 min. The optimal mass ratio between naked dsRNA and the nanomaterials, ensuring complete loading, was determined using a 1 % agarose gel. This ratio ranges from PEI-SWNT:dsRNA at 0.1:1 to 0.3:1 (w/w), PEI-CQDs:dsRNA from 1:1 to 4:1 (w/w), and MgAl-LDH:dsRNA from 1:1 to 6:1 (w/w). The binding ratio was calculated as follows:
Binding ratio = 1 −
Densitometric value of the unbound dsRNA band Densitometric value of the dsRNA band in the absence of nanomaterials

For pH-dependent stability assessment, 500 ng of dsRNA and either 100 ng of PEI-SWNT (or 1500 ng of PEI-CQDs, 3000 ng of MgAl-LDH) were combined in water adjusted to pH 4, 7, and 10, followed by a 30-min incubation at 28 °C. Subsequently, the samples were analyzed on 1 % agarose gel. To investigate temperature-dependent stability, 500 ng of dsRNA was mixed with the optimal nanomaterial ratio in a tube and then incubated at 28 °C for 30 min. Following this, the samples were individually exposed to temperatures of 4, 28, and 37 °C for varying durations (1, 5, or 10 days). To assess the stability of dsRNA/nanomaterials in midgut fluid, fluid from five N3D3 locust nymphs was collected and dissolved in 100 μL Ringer solution, and then midgut fluid (20 μg of total protein) was mixed with dsRNA and nanomaterials/dsRNA at 28 °C for 5–120 min. To further verify whether the bright band in the gel well represent intact dsRNA, the samples were mixed with 0.1 % SDS subjected to varying temperatures and incubation times in midgut fluid (Castellanos et al., 2019), and analyzed by 2 % agarose gel (PEI-SWNT/dsRNA, PEI-CQDs/dsRNA) or 1 % agarose gel (MgAl-LDH/dsRNA).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) imaging of PEI-SWNT/dsRNA
ce:section.ce:para: PEI-SWNT was utilized as a negative control in this experiment. The PEI-SWNT/dsRNA and PEI-SWNT samples were deposited onto clean copper grids. Subsequently, 2 % phosphotungstic acid was used for natural staining, followed by incubation at 25 °C for 10–20 min. After staining, the samples were allowed to dry naturally. Imaging was performed using a JEOL 2010F transmission electron microscope to analyze the morphology of the samples.

The hydrodynamic diameters (Z-average) and zeta (ζ) potentials (surface charges) of the PEI-SWNT/dsRNA complexes (contain 1 ng/μL dsRNA) and PEI-SWNT solutions were measured using a Malvern Zetasizer Nano-ZS. The measurements were conducted at 25 °C and repeated three times to ensure reliability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Injection of nanomaterial/dsRNAs in L. migratoria
ce:section.ce:para: In this study, one day-third instar locust nymphs (N3D1) were selected for subsequent experiments. To determine the optimal injection concentration of dsRNA, 5 ng, 50 ng, and 100 ng of dsLmCht10, or 1 ng, 5 ng of dsLmATP5A, were respectively injected into the hemolymph of Locusta. Subsequently, 5 locusts were randomly selected for RNA extraction and real-time quantitative PCR analysis, while the remaining 10–15 locusts were observed for phenotypic changes. Finally, 5 ng of dsLmCht10 and 1 ng dsLmATP5A were selected and used. For the injection of nanomaterials/dsRNA complexes, a solution containing 5 ng of dsLmCht10 and 1 ng of PEI-SWNT (or a solution containing 1 ng of dsLmATP5A and 0.2 ng of PEI-SWNT) was injected into the second or third abdominal segment of locusts using a micro-syringe. And all experiments were repeated at least three times.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Extract RNA from L. migratoria and perform RT-qPCR analysis
ce:section.ce:para: To assess the efficiency of RNAi in locusts treated with nanomaterial/dsRNA, RNA was extracted from locusts, followed by Reverse transcription quantitative polymerase chain reaction (RT-qPCR) analysis. RNA was extracted using the Trizol method (RNA isoTM Plus, TaKaRa, Cat: 9109). For the subsequent experiments, 1 μg of RNA was reverse transcribed to cDNA using the HiScript® III RT SuperMix (+gDNA wiper) (Vamyze, Cat: L/N 7E691l2). Based on the cDNA, qRT-PCR was performed using the ChamQ Universal SYBR qPCR Master Mix (Vamyze, Cat: Q711–02). The reaction mixture of qPCR contained 1 μL of cDNA, 10 μL of enzyme, 0.5 μL each of forward and reverse primers, and 8 μL of DNase/RNase-free water (Solarbio, Cat: R1600). The EF1α gene was used as the control gene. Data was analyzed and calculated using the 2-ΔΔCt method. Each experiment was repeated three times.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Measurement of wheat germination rate and seedling length
ce:section.ce:para: Prior to the experiment, seeds were washed 4–5 times with water and placed in plastic culture dishes. Solutions of PEI-SWNT at concentrations of 1, 40, and 80 ng/μL were prepared with ultrapure water, adjusted to a total volume of 10 mL, and evenly distributed among the seeds in each group. The number of germinated seeds was counted and recorded for further analysis after 3 days.

Additionally, the impact of PEI-SWNT on the length of wheat seedlings was assessed. Wheat seedlings were grown hydroponically in ultrapure water until reaching 2–3 cm in height. The same treatment groups (1, 40, and 80 ng/μL PEI-SWNT) were applied using the previously described method. Seedling growth was monitored daily, with ultrapure water replenished as needed. The heights of seedlings in all groups were measured with a ruler and recorded for further analysis after 4 days.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Feeding of PEI-SWNT/dsRNA in L. migratoria
ce:section.ce:para: In this study, one day-fifth instar locust nymphs (N5D1) were selected for subsequent experiments. In the groups designated for oral delivery, locust nymphs were fed fresh wheat seedlings that had been sprayed with the solution of PEI-SWNT/dsRNA complexes. Each locust nymph was fed 25 μg of dsRNA and 5 μg of PEI-SWNT every day.

Due to the ineffectiveness of direct PEI-SWNT/dsRNA feeding in downregulating the expression of the target gene, we hypothesized that dsRNase2 in the locusts may degrade the dsRNA. Consequently, we injected 15 μg of dsLmdsRNase2 to reduce dsRNase2 expression. After a 30-h interval, we then fed the locusts fresh wheat infused with dsRNA and nanomaterials/dsRNA. After 3 days, five locusts were selected for RNA extraction and analysis of RNAi efficiency, while the remaining 10–15 locusts in each group were monitored for phenotypic changes. All experiments were conducted with three repetitions for each group and data set.

In the experiment, data were shown as the mean ± standard error of the mean (SEM). Analysis was implemented using ANOVA followed by Tukey's test for multiple comparsion. Differences between groups were considered significant when the P-value was less than 0.05.

In this study, polyethyleneimine (PEI), a cationic polymer known for its easy modification and good biocompatibility, was used to modify carbon nanotubes and carbon quantum dots to impart positive charges, enabling electrostatic attraction and self-assembly with dsRNA. LDH was synthesized via coprecipitation with Mg2+, Al3+, and NaOH, and subsequently self-assembled with dsRNA (Fig. 1A).

To explore the loading capacities of different nanomaterials for dsRNA, experiments were conducted using dsLmCht10, a 529 bp dsRNA molecule. Gel electrophoresis was employed to determine the optimal mass ratios of dsRNA to various nanomaterials. Upon complete binding of dsRNA to nanomaterials, no migrating dsRNA bands were observed in the gel lanes, indicating accumulation of dsRNA-loaded nanomaterials at the gel well. Conversely, migrating dsRNA bands were visible when incomplete binding occurred. For instance, PEI-SWNT/dsRNA nanoparticles prepared at a mass ratio of 0.2:1 showed no migrating dsRNA bands, demonstrating complete binding of dsRNA to PEI-SWNT (Fig. 1B and S1). When PEI-CQDs were bound to dsRNA, bands at mass ratios of 1:1 and 2:1 exhibited reduced brightness, while at 3:1, almost no residual dsRNA bands were visible, indicating complete binding (Fig. 1B and S1). Similarly, with LDH, dsRNA bands in gel lanes diminished gradually with mass ratios ranging from 0:1 to 4:1, until complete binding was achieved at 5:1 (Fig. 1B and S1).

Subsequently, experiments were conducted using dsLmATP5A, a 413 bp dsRNA molecule, maintaining mass ratios of 0.2:1 and 3:1 for PEI-SWNT:dsRNA and PEI-CQDs:dsRNA, respectively, while adjusting the ratio to 6:1 for LDH:dsRNA (Fig. S1). In this study, we observed that three types of nanomaterials can effectively bind to dsRNA. Notably, the PEI-SWNT required the least amount to achieve binding with the same quantity of dsRNA.

The ability of nanomaterials to avoid degradation of dsRNA by nucleases is a critical factor influencing RNAi efficiency (Dhandapani et al., 2019). To investigate the protective capabilities of three inorganic nanomaterials against dsRNA in locusts, nanomaterial/dsRNA particles were exposed to midgut fluid (20 μg of total protein) extracted from third-instar locusts and digested for 0–120 min. Naked dsRNA was completely degraded within 5 min in the midgut fluid (Fig. 1C). Due to the nanomaterials' entrapment of dsRNA within pores, preventing direct observation of dsRNA digestion, we isolated dsRNA from nanomaterial/dsRNA particles using 0.1 % SDS. As shown in Fig. 1C, PEI-SWNT and PEI-CQDs protected dsRNA from degradation by nucleases in the midgut fluid for up to 120 min, whereas dsRNA in LDH/dsRNA was degraded within 5 min. These results indicate that compared to LDH, PEI-SWNT and PEI-CQDs offer superior protection to dsRNA, thereby extending its functionality in the presence of nucleases. Analysis of dsRNA bands released from PEI-SWNT/dsRNA and PEI-CQDs/dsRNA after 120 min revealed that PEI-SWNT preserved 57.9 % of dsRNA from nuclease degradation, whereas PEI-CQDs preserved 36.3 % within the same timeframe. This suggests that PEI-SWNT enhances dsRNA stability against nucleases more effectively than PEI-CQDs.

To explore the storage stability of PEI-SWNT/dsRNA and PEI-CQDs/dsRNA complexes, nanomaterial/dsRNA composites were stored at 4 °C, 28 °C, and 37 °C for 1, 5, and 10 days, respectively. Subsequently, dsRNA was separated from the nanomaterials using 0.1 % SDS, and the residual dsRNA was analyzed by gel electrophoresis. As shown in Fig. 1D, at 4 °C, both naked dsRNA and the PEI-SWNT/dsRNA and PEI-CQDs/dsRNA complexes remained largely undegraded over 1–10 days of storage. However, at 28 °C and 37 °C, naked dsRNA was nearly completely degraded by day 5 and day 1, respectively. At 28 °C, PEI-SWNT and PEI-CQDs protected dsRNA from degradation. At 37 °C, PEI-SWNT effectively preserved dsRNA integrity over 1–10 days, whereas the dsRNA combined with PEI-CQDs shows significant degradation after 10 days. These results indicate that PEI-SWNT enhances dsRNA stability more effectively at different temperatures compared to PEI-CQDs.

Further, we adjusted the water to different pH levels, such as pH 4.0, 7.0, and 10.0, and incubated three types of inorganic nanomaterials in water of varying pH with dsRNA (500 ng) at 28 °C for 30 min to assess their binding capacity. The results showed that under different pH conditions, PEI-SWNT, PEI-CQDs, and LDH all exhibited complete binding with dsRNA (Fig. S2), demonstrating the stability of the nanomaterial/dsRNA complexes.

In summary, compared to PEI-CQDs and LDH, PEI-SWNT showed superior protection of dsRNA from degradation in both midgut fluid and at different temperatures. Moreover, the binding of PEI-SWNT to dsRNA was unaffected by pH, highlighting its effectiveness as a robust nanocarrier.

To investigate the morphology of PEI-SWNT/dsRNA, TEM experiments were conducted, with PEI-SWNT morphology serving as a control. The results revealed that the control group of PEI-SWNT exhibited an elongated shape. In contrast, upon binding with dsRNA, the dsRNA was observed to attach directly around the PEI-SWNT (Fig. 2A). In addition, we employed DLS to measure the hydrodynamic sizes (Z-average) and zeta potentials (surface charge) of PEI-SWNT/dsLmCht10 solution, PEI-SWNT solution, and dsLmCht10 solution. According to DLS measurements, the average hydrodynamic diameter of dsLmCht10 solution was 311.3 d.nm, PEI-SWNT solution showed an average hydrodynamic diameter of 186.1 d.nm, and PEI-SWNT/dsLmCht10 exhibited an average hydrodynamic diameter of 571.3 d.nm (Fig. 2B). This indicates an increase in particle size due to dsRNA attachment to the surface of PEI-SWNT. Given the presence of the cationic polymer PEI, PEI-SWNT displayed a positive zeta potential of 35.33 ± 6.07 mV, whereas dsLmCht10 exhibited a negative zeta potential of −10.1 ± 1.1 mV. Upon binding with dsLmCht10, the zeta potential of PEI-SWNT/dsRNA decreased by 7.0 ± 0.8 mV (Fig. 2C). This finding confirms an overall positive surface charge for PEI-SWNT/dsRNA complexes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of PEI-SWNT/dsRNA nanoparticle complex on L. migratoria development
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the efficacy of PEI-SWNT/dsRNA nanocomplexes in silencing target genes and their insecticidal effects in locusts, we selected two genes for experimentation: LmCht10, associated with molting in locust nymphs (Yang et al., 2016), and LmATP5A, an essential α-subunit gene of F1-ATP synthase (Hu and Xia, 2016).

In this study, one day-third instar nymphs (N3D1) of locusts were injected with dsLmCht10, and observed over 4–5 days for molting difficulties and subsequent mortality, as well as injected with dsLmATP5A and monitored for mortality within 24 h post-injection. To determine optimal dsRNA concentrations, locust nymphs were injected with 0, 5, 50, or 100 ng of dsLmCht10, and 0, 1, or 5 ng of dsLmATP5A, followed by qPCR to assess target gene silencing efficiency 24 h post-injection, alongside monitoring of nymph mortality over appropriate durations. The results indicated that compared to injection of 0 ng dsLmCht10, injection of 5 ng dsLmCht10 reduced the expression level of LmCht10 in locusts by 32.5 %, with a subsequent mortality rate of 46.7 % after 5 days (Fig. 3A and B). Injection of 50 ng and 100 ng dsLmCht10 resulted in a significant reduction of LmCht10 expression by 75.2 % and 93.2 % in locusts, respectively, accompanied by mortality rates of 86.7 % and 100 % (Fig. 3A and B). To better demonstrate the efficiency of RNAi and mortality rate following the binding of nanomaterial PEI-SWNT with dsRNA in locusts, we selected 5 ng dsLmCht10 for subsequent experiments. Similarly, following the injection of 1 ng dsLmATP5A, there was no significant difference in LmATP5A expression observed at 24 h post-injection when compared to the 0 ng dsLmATP5A group, which exhibited a mortality rate of only 6.5 %. In contrast, the group injected with 5 ng dsLmATP5A exhibited a silencing rate of 77.3 % and a high mortality rate of 46.7 % (Fig. S3A and S3B). Therefore, we chose to inject 1 ng dsLmATP5A for further experiments to better demonstrate the enhancement in RNAi efficiency and mortality rate following nanomaterial binding with dsRNA.

To assess the silencing efficiency of PEI-SWNT/dsRNA nanoparticles targeting LmCht10 and LmATP5A genes, dsGFP, naked dsRNA, PEI-SWNT, and PEI-SWNT/dsRNA were injected into N3D1 locust nymphs, and mRNA expression levels of LmCht10 and LmATP5A were analyzed 24 h post-injection. No significant differences in gene expression were observed between the dsGFP and PEI-SWNT control groups (Fig. 3C and S3C). Subsequently, the gene silencing capabilities of naked dsRNA versus PEI-SWNT/dsRNA were compared in locusts. Compared to naked dsRNA, as shown in Fig. 3C and S3C, PEI-SWNT/dsLmCht10 and PEI-SWNT/dsLmATP5A achieved enhanced silencing efficiencies of 36.5 % and 46.0 %, respectively, indicating superior gene knockdown effectiveness of PEI-SWNT/dsRNA nanoparticles over naked dsRNA.

To further assess the RNAi efficiency of PEI-SWNT/dsRNA nanoparticles, we measured the mortality rates of locusts 4 to 5 days after the injection of PEI-SWNT/dsLmCht10 (containing 1 ng PEI-SWNT and 5 ng dsLmCht10) during molting, as well as 24 h after the injection of PEI-SWNT/dsLmATP5A (containing 0.2 ng PEI-SWNT and 1 ng dsLmATP5A). When LmCht10 was silenced, locusts exhibited abnormal molting, ultimately leading to death. Silencing of LmATP5A also resulted in locust mortality. Compared to locusts injected with naked dsRNA, mortality rates significantly increased by 23.4 % for PEI-SWNT/dsLmCht10 (Fig. 3D) and 39.0 % for PEI-SWNT/dsLmATP5A (Fig. S3D). Conversely, no mortality was observed in locusts injected with dsGFP or PEI-SWNT controls (Fig. 3D and S3D). Additionally, the PEI-SWNT/dsLmCht10 group accelerated locust mortality, showing a 42.7 % increase in premature deaths by day four compared to the naked dsRNA group (Fig. 3E). Thus, our study further confirms that PEI-SWNT/dsRNA nanoparticles exhibit more effective RNAi-mediated silencing and knockdown of LmCht10 and LmATP5A genes compared to naked dsRNA.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Feeding locusts with PEI-SWNT/dsRNA via leaf spraying
ce:section.ce:para: To further assess the practical application potential of PEI-SWNT/dsRNA nanocomposites as nucleic acid pesticides, we combined PEI-SWNT (80 ng/μL) with dsLmCht10 (2.5 μg/μL) at a 0.2:1 mass ratio and sprayed it onto wheat leaves to feed N5D1 instar locusts. Each locust received 25 μg of dsLmCht10 and 5 μg of PEI-SWNT daily for 3 consecutive days. If the locusts died due to gene silencing of LmCht10, they would exhibit a molting-deficient death phenotype (Fig. 4A). Unexpectedly, the expression level of the LmCht10 gene in the PEI-SWNT/dsLmCht10 group did not differ significantly from the control group when directly fed (Fig. S4A). Subsequently, the locusts were then allowed to feed until molting from fifth instar to adult with normal molting. We hypothesize that dsRNase2 in the locust gut might be too actively degrading the dsRNA. Subsequently, N5D1 instar locusts were injected with 15 μg dsLmdsRNase2, and 30 h later, they were fed with wheat leaves sprayed with PEI-SWNT/dsLmCht10. After continuous feeding for 3 days, we assessed the transcriptional levels of the LmdsRNase2 gene. The results showed that locusts injected with dsLmdsRNase2 exhibited a significantly reduced expression of dsRNase2 (Fig. S4B). The expression level of LmCht10 in locusts fed with PEI-SWNT/dsLmCht10 was significantly lower compared to the control group, whereas locusts fed with dsLmCht10 showed no significant difference in LmCht10 expression from the control group (Fig. 4B). Statistical analysis of locust mortality revealed that the death rate in the PEI-SWNT/dsLmCht10 group was 3.5 times higher than that in the dsLmCht10 group (Fig. 4C). Subsequently, we injected dsLmdsRNase2 and conducted a 3-day feeding experiment, during which locusts were continuously fed with either PEI-SWNT/dsLmATP5 A or dsLmATP5 A. We assessed the expression of the target gene and the mortality rate. Consistent with the results from feeding with PEI-SWNT/dsLmCht10, LmATP5A expression was significantly lower in locusts fed with PEI-SWNT/dsLmATP5A compared to controls, while no difference was observed in locusts fed with dsLmATP5A (Fig. S4C). Compared to feeding with dsLmATP5A, feeding with PEI-SWNT/dsLmATP5A resulted in a 6.7 % increase in locust mortality. Our study further confirms that PEI-SWNT/dsRNA-based nanoparticles are more effective for gene silencing compared to naked dsRNA.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The impact of PEI-SWNT on wheat
ce:section.ce:para: The application of nanoparticles via foliar spraying or soil amendment can have significant implications for plant growth in the environment. Seed germination indices provide a sensitive assessment of the potential toxicity and bioavailability of substances in the environment to plants. This study focuses on wheat seeds, investigating the effects of PEI-SWNT at concentrations of 1, 40, and 80 ng/μL on seed germination rates over a three-day period. The results demonstrate that PEI-SWNT at concentrations of 40 ng/μL and 80 ng/μL significantly enhanced wheat seed germination compared to the control, whereas the 1 ng/μL SWNT treatment group showed no significant difference from the control group (Fig. 5A and B). These findings indicate that low concentrations of PEI-SWNT don't affect wheat seed germination, while concentrations of 40–80 ng/μL have a promoting effect on seed germination. Subsequently, we selected 4-day-old seedlings of comparable growth and treated them with PEI-SWNT at concentrations of 0, 1, 40, and 80 ng/μL. Four days later, we measured the height of the seedlings. The results revealed no significant differences in seedling length between the 1, 40, and 80 ng/μL PEI-SWNT treatments and the control group (Fig. 5C).

ce:section.ce:section-title: PEI-SWNT exhibits superior binding affinity and protective capacity for dsRNA
ce:section.ce:para: RNAi has demonstrated significant potential in agricultural pest control due to its simplicity and specificity (Bramlett et al., 2020; Zotti et al., 2018). However, the instability of dsRNA in the environment affects RNAi efficiency and its ability to control pests. Nanomaterials, as a new class of carriers, can deliver nucleic acids into cells and protect them from degradation (Yang et al., 2022). In contrast, the application of inorganic nanomaterials in pest control is less explored. Currently, no studies have compared the potential of inorganic nanomaterials for application in locusts.

In this study, we screened three positively charged inorganic nanoparticles—PEI-SWNT, PEI-CQDs, and LDH—and evaluated their binding properties with negatively charged dsRNA in locusts. We evaluated the binding quality ratio of the nanoparticles to dsRNA, as well as their protective effects on dsRNA in locust gut fluid, under different temperatures and pH conditions. Similar to previous studies, dsRNA can be protected from degradation by nucleases when combined with nano-carriers such as functionalized carbon dots (Wang et al., 2023) and star-shaped polymers (Ma et al., 2022). We hypothesize that this protection may result from dsRNA adhering to the surface of nanomaterials, thereby reducing binding sites available for nucleases. The excellent storage stability and sustained release capabilities observed with PEI-SWNT suggest its potential as a reliable carrier for dsRNA, facilitating pest control effects.

ce:section.ce:section-title: PEI-SWNT could significantly enhance the efficiency of RNAi
ce:section.ce:para: PEI-SWNT is commonly employed as a carrier for delivering plasmid DNA into plant cells (Demirer et al., 2019). However, the research on the application of carbon nanotubes in pest control is limited. We tested the surface charge of PEI-SWNT/dsRNA and found it to be positively charged (7.0 ± 0.8 mV). Positively charged particles have a strong affinity for the negatively charged surfaces of cell membranes, which enhances cellular uptake (Lu et al., 2022).

The injection and feeding experiment further confirmed this point. Compared to the dsRNA injection group, injecting PEI-SWNT/dsRNA into third-instar locusts improved gene silencing efficiency and locust mortality rates, showing increases of 36.5 %–46.0 % and 23.4 %–39.0 %, respectively. Compared to the dsRNA feeding group, feeding PEI-SWNT/dsRNA resulted in a 3.5-fold increase in locust mortality. Research has demonstrated the application of functionalized carbon nanotubes for the delivery of dsRNA in Tribolium castaneum, significantly enhancing the RNAi efficiency of target genes (Edwards et al., 2020). The use of PEI-SWNT/dsRNA in genetic research and pest management can reduce the dosage of dsRNA and lower costs simultaneously.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Limitations and future outlook of PEI-SWNT application in locusts
ce:section.ce:para: Previous research has shown that injecting 50 mg/L PEI-SWNT into the leaves of dicot plants, rather than SWNT, is toxic to Arabidopsis. However, toxicity is reduced when different polycation modifications are used on SWNT (González-Grandío et al., 2021). It remains unclear how much PEI-SWNT is absorbed by monocotyledonous plants through their roots and what impact this absorption has on their growth. We investigated that PEI-SWNT at concentrations of 40–80 ng/μL enhances wheat germination rates without affecting seedling length in the short term. These findings are consistent with previous studies on carbon-based nanomaterials (Verma et al., 2019). It may be due to carbon nanotubes enhancing the water retention capacity of tomato seeds, thereby promoting seed germination. (Khodakovskaya et al., 2009). Future research should thoroughly analyze the absorption rate of PEI-SWNT in wheat and the expression levels of various immune-related genes to comprehensively assess the safety of PEI-SWNT for wheat.

Using PEI-SWNT/dsRNA nanocomplexes for feeding locusts still requires preemptively reducing LmdsRNase2 expression to better protect dsRNA. Only one study has demonstrated that using other copolymers to deliver dsRNA through direct feeding could increase locust mortality rates to 10 %, but the mortality rate remains limited (Lu et al., 2022). Future studies should focus on identifying more stable, biocompatible, and dsRNase-resistant nanomaterials that can provide enhanced protection for dsRNA under both environmental conditions and within locusts, while ensuring that the release of dsRNA remains unaffected. Alternatively, in subsequent studies, our laboratory would explore various dsRNase2 inhibitors with the aim of combining them with nanomaterials/dsRNA, so that pest control could be achieved through feeding without the pre-injection of dsLmdsRNase2.

In conclusion, this study developed a PEI-SWNT-based dsRNA delivery system, providing a practical example of nanoparticle-mediated RNAi. The PEI-SWNT /dsRNA complex effectively protects dsRNA from degradation and demonstrates good storage stability. Both injection and feeding of PEI-SWNT /dsRNA significantly reduced the expression of target genes, and improved mortality, proving to be more effective in gene silencing compared to naked dsRNA. Moreover, an appropriate amount of PEI-SWNT enhanced the germination rate of wheat without adversely affecting its growth, highlighting the feasibility of using nanomaterials for dsRNA delivery in managing locusts and offering a new strategy for pest management.

Qiurong Ren: Writing – original draft, Data curation, Conceptualization. Qian Zhang: Validation, Data curation. Yangyang Liu: Validation, Methodology. Shuai Li: Validation, Methodology. Jianqin Zhang: Project administration, Methodology, Conceptualization. Yanli Wang: Writing – original draft, Conceptualization. Abeer El Wakil: Writing – review & editing, Project administration, Funding acquisition. Bernard Moussian: Writing – review & editing, Project administration. Jianzhen Zhang: Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare no competing financial interests.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by National Key R&D Program of China (2022YFE0196200), Science, Technology & Innovation Funding (STDF) Authority in Egypt (46567), Taishan Industrial Leading Talent Program, and Postgraduate Education Innovation Program of Shanxi Province (2024TD05).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.pestbp.2025.106361.
